data.title: The 'Real' Economy Is In Real Trouble
data.description:
The massive surge in tech spending with the AI infrastructure buildout continues to power the U.S. economy. This has led to a quite bifurcated economic landscape. I am struggling to remember another time where there was such a disconnect between the headline GDP numbers and the "real" economy.

After a slight contraction in the first quarter, largely due to companies front-loading imports to avoid coming tariffs, GDP growth in Q2 came in at a robust 3.9%. The most recent forecast from the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow has 4.2% GDP growth penciled in for Q3.

Of course, a huge portion of this GDP growth is being driven by the huge surge of tech spending for AI infrastructure. The capital expenditure budget at Amazon (AMZN) in FY2026 will be roughly three times that of FY2023. Other tech giants like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META) are making similar financial commitments.

Relative to GDP, spending on AI has already surpassed the peak of the Internet Boom. We are not to the peak of the railroad boom from 150 years ago yet. However, from a depreciation basis, that has been achieved as railroad components (track, engines, boxcars, etc..) have a much longer useful life than a GPU. And remember that previous massive misallocation of capital led directly to the Panic of 1873 and more than four years of recession.

The huge amount of fiscal deficit spending is also contributing to GDP growth. However, given the U.S.'s debt-to-GDP ratio, how long this is sustainable is an open question. The military industrial complex is doing well thanks to the proxy war in Ukraine and the huge boost in defense spending in the FY2026 federal budget.

Outside of these areas, it is difficult to find many economic engines that are not sputtering right now, as the "real" economy is either in a recession or getting close to it. Housing-related activity makes up 15% to 18% of GDP. As I recently highlighted, the pain in the moribund housing sector is just getting started, and 2026 is shaping up to be a hard year for home sellers.

Housing affordability remains historically low, and average mortgage rates remain stubbornly above the 6% threshold despite 125bps worth of reductions to the Fed Funds rate since the summer of 2024. 2026 is about to commence with the number of home sellers over home buyers at their highest levels in more than a decade. A reading this week from Zillow shows that the average home listing experienced record-breaking cumulative discounts of up to $25,000 in October.

Then we have the commercial real estate or CRE. Multi-family and the Office sectors are really struggling right now. These account for approximately $3.5 trillion of the roughly $4.8 trillion in CRE debt outstanding. The delinquency rate for Commercial Mortgage Back Securities or CMBS on office properties has surpassed the peak of the Great Financial Crisis and is close to 12%. And this does not account for the considerable amount of 'extend & pretend' loan modifications that have taken place by lenders.

CMBS delinquency rates around multi-family (around $2.2 trillion in outstanding debt) have just more than doubled over the past 13-14 months to near 7%. With median rents flatlining nationally and falling in many major metro regions, while costs (insurance, maintenance, taxes, staffing, etc.) continue to rise at least at the pace of overall inflation, the situation here is going to get worse before it gets better.

Then we have the jobs market, which clearly has started to deteriorate in recent months as I documented again in an article earlier this week. Major companies like United Parcel Service (UPS) and Amazon have announced significant new layoffs recently, and October had the largest number (153K) of layoff announcements of any October since 2003. Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) just announced it would lay off approximately 10% of its employee base in the coming quarters.

Despite the huge surge of spending on data center construction and infrastructure, it hasn't been enough to trigger a rise in the U.S. Leading Economic Indicators Index, which continues to decline despite the robust quarterly GDP figures.

Today, the Chicago PMI reading came in at 36.3 in November, from 43.8 in October. This is compared with the 44.3 consensus. Anything below a 50 reading is recessionary, it should be noted.

Then we have the state of the consumer, which drives roughly two-thirds of GDP growth. Even with recent 4% headline GDP growth, a recent survey showed roughly one-quarter of all Americans are living paycheck to paycheck.

Sub-prime auto loans 60 days or more past due hit 6.65% in October. That is a higher delinquency rate than the peak seen in the Great Financial Crisis. Student loan payments and collections were reinstated earlier this year, after a nearly four-year taxpayer funded hiatus. Not surprisingly, student loan delinquency rates are soaring. Nearly 13% of the just under $1.8 billion in student loan debt is now seriously past due.

Student loans are also now again being reported to credit bureaus, significantly lowering credit scores for millions of student loan holders. This depletes the potential buying pool for big ticket items like homes, autos, appliances, boats, and RVs.

Finally, total credit card debt continues to rise. It hit an all-time high of $1.33 trillion in October. Given the faltering jobs and housing market, debt loads, and rising delinquencies, is it any wonder consumer sentiment is in the tank?

This is the $64,000 question for investors. Can the market continue to trade at extreme valuation levels viewed from several historical valuation metrics when the economy is primarily being held up by the AI Revolution and much of the "real" economy is in recession or close to being so?

This is something we are going to find out together as we get into 2026.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nutanix: Buy The Dip As Free Cash Flow Rises (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we head into the final month of 2025, our minds should be focused on how to protect our portfolios from downside in 2026. Stock market multiples are at multi-year highs, and in my view, the best way to diversify is by rotating our portfolios into "growth at a reasonable price" stocks that have their own catalysts to outperform the S&P 500.

Often, the best plays here are rebound names. Nutanix, Inc. (NTNX) is emerging as a strong rebound candidate after a very poorly received fiscal Q1 earnings release, which featured a cut to full-year revenue expectations. Shares of Nutanix sank nearly 20% after the release, wiping out all of the stock's YTD gains.

I last wrote a neutral article on Nutanix in September, when the stock was trading briefly above $80. At the time, I had called out the company's strong execution as being more than compensated for by high valuation multiples.

Today, Nutanix is sitting ~40% lower than where it was at the time of my prior article, and investors are concerned about its growth trends: the exact opposite of the situation previously. It's certainly true that once software stocks enter the penalty box, it will require some patience to dig out. That said, I like the fact that the company is still forecasting strong bookings, while also boosting its cash flow expectations. I'm upgrading Nutanix to a Buy rating.

Let's start with the elephant in the room: Nutanix's guidance cut. For the remainder of FY26, which is the fiscal year ending in July 2026, Nutanix is guiding to $2.82-$2.86 billion in revenue, which represents 11-13% y/y growth and a cut from $2.90-$2.94 billion, or 14-15% growth previously.

Importantly, however, management comments that the reduction isn't necessarily a poor demand signal. Instead, it appears while the company is expecting the same in full-year bookings, customers are looking to push out license timing and adoption to later dates, which is causing Nutanix to believe that more deals signed near fiscal year-end will end up pushing out to FY27. Obviously, we'd like to get the revenue sooner: but at the same time, it would be worse if Nutanix was reporting a guidance cut due to weaker demand, poorer win rates, or greater competitive activity. It's none of the above.

Furthermore, we like the fact that Nutanix actually boosted its FCF guidance to $800-$840 million, or a 1% bump at the midpoint versus a previous $790-$830 million range; and that the stock is now trading at healthy multiples of free cash flow.

At current share prices near $49, Nutanix trades at a market cap of $13.11 billion. Netting off the $1.99 billion of cash against $1.34 billion of convertible debt on Nutanix's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $12.46 billion.

This positions Nutanix's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 15.2x EV/FY26 FCF.

This is the first time in recent memory that Nutanix has traded at an appropriate mid-teens multiple of FCF; furthermore, Nutanix's revenue multiple is now also closer in line with peers like NetApp (NTAP), which is growing at a much slower single digit pace, while trading cheaper than Pure Storage (PSTG).

In my view, investors now have a safer entry point to buy the dip here.

Q1 download

Let's now assess Nutanix's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Nutanix's revenue grew 13% y/y to $670.6 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $676.6 million (+15% y/y) by a two-point margin. Meanwhile, revenue growth also decelerated six points versus 19% y/y growth in Q4.

Again, Nutanix mainly chalks this up to license start timing rather than to deal weakness. No, Nutanix's revenue deceleration doesn't necessarily signal that the AI investment cycle into data centers, which is lifting spending across the infrastructure stack on products like Nutanix, is slowing down.

Per CFO Rukmini Sivaraman's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

While bookings in Q1 were slightly ahead of our expectations, we saw a larger-than-expected proportion of land and expand bookings with future start dates late in the quarter, resulting in a shift of some revenue from Q1 into future periods.

As a reminder, under U.S. GAAP, revenue recognition generally begins when the license starts, which means bookings with future start dates shift revenue recognition into later periods, even though cash collection may occur earlier. This is solely timing related and does not change the overall revenue expected to be recognized over time. If land and expand bookings had come in with the proportion of future start dates that we had assumed, Q1 revenue would have been above the high end of the guided range."

There are several metrics that substantial management's claim that the revenue slowdown and guidance cut were just timing-based. Billings, which is recognized when deals are signed and not when licenses start, grew 17% y/y to $708.1 million, far outpacing revenue growth.

Furthermore, Nutanix's remaining performance obligations, or essentially backlog, also grew 26% y/y to a whopping $2.67 billion. Meanwhile, the current portion of this backlog, which will be recognized as revenue within the next 12 months, grew 17% y/y to $1.30 billion. The fact that all of these metrics outpace revenue growth is a strong signal that actual deal activity is healthy, and that revenue growth shouldn't decelerate much further.

We note as well that Nutanix added $82 million of net-new ARR to end Q1 with $2.28 billion, further increasing the stability of its software business and adding more in ARR compared to $70 million in Q1 of last year. All of these metrics suggest that in spite of a tougher macroeconomic landscape in which many companies have noted IT budget tightness, what Nutanix is experiencing is not a loss of deals but rather a deferral in start times.

Pro forma operating margins also held roughly flat y/y at 19.7%. Meanwhile, Nutanix's cash flow is becoming increasingly critical in defining the stock's valuation and its bull case, and FCF grew 15% y/y to $174.5 million.

We note that Nutanix's guidance for FY26 actually only calls for 9% y/y FCF growth, so with a healthy start to the fiscal year the company could end up raising its FCF guidance further.

Key takeaways

Investors are clearly nervous amid sky-high markets, and that skittishness is playing out in oversized post-earnings reactions when results are slightly unexpected. We won't argue here that Nutanix's deal timing deferrals aren't an issue, but I do think that the presence of other healthy indicators, like healthy billings and strong RPO expansion, help to justify buying the dip in Nutanix as its free cash flow multiple compresses down to very reasonable levels.

Take advantage of the NTNX stock dip here to take a position.

data.title: Touchstone Small Cap Fund Q3 2025 Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Touchstone Small Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The Touchstone Small Cap Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) (TSFAX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000® Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.

Among the largest contributors to Fund performance were Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) (Industrials sector), Ingevity Corporation (NGVT) (Materials sector), and Graham Holdings Co. (GHC) (Consumer Discretionary sector).

Armstrong World Industries, Inc. shares outperformed in the quarter due to beating expectations, driven by better than expected volumes, favorable positioning in key verticals, and strong operating leverage. We continue to like Armstrong for its consistent execution, strong financials, leading market share and persistent moats through its exclusivity agreements and warranties.

Ingevity Corporation outperformed its benchmark after showing a significant margin recovery in recent earnings, exceeding low expectations. It cleared major headwinds in its Performance Chemicals segment, fueling year-over-year gains. The strategic portfolio shifts are driving meaningful profit recovery for the next few years. While much of the business is experiencing a cyclical downturn, the overall mix of revenue is improving, as performance materials makes up a larger portion of the pie.

Graham Holdings Co. outperformed the benchmark on strong second quarter results, led by solid growth in Kaplan Education and the continued expansion of its Healthcare segment. The stock likely benefited from the perceived easing of broadcast TV merger and acquisition regulation, which could enable future station monetization or local consolidation. We remain positive on the Graham Holdings family’s fiduciary leadership, strong balance sheet, and track record of returning capital to shareholders.

Among the largest detractors from Fund performance were Interparfums, Inc. (IPAR) (Consumer Discretionary sector), Haemonetics Corporation (HAE)(Health Care sector), and White Mountains Insurance Group, Ltd. (WTM)(Financials sector).

Interparfums, Inc. was a bottom holding as retailers are managing inventory more cautiously and the overall fragrance market has softened. Interparfum’s brands continue to be resilient with solid growth and incremental share gains. We remain confident in its ability to outgrow its end market through rising adoption and strong execution in innovation plus brand partnerships.

Haemonetics Corporation was a bottom performer after reporting a mixed quarter, with the price reaction seemingly extrapolating forward near-term headwinds. Its vascular closure business is facing some elevated competitive pressures due to legacy vendors reacting more aggressively to the perceived threat from its business. However, we believe the company’s portfolio of clinically differentiated, high-margin products will win market share and drive robust earnings growth over time. The thesis relies on minimal improvement in the core segments to drive strong investor returns.

White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd was a bottom name due to industry headwinds and investment volatility in its portfolio. Concerns about residuals from early catastrophe hits have also pressured the stock. We remain confident in the company’s ability to deliver outsized growth in book value per share over time through prudent capital allocation.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of small-cap companies, which may be subject to more erratic market movements than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Advisor engages a sub-advisor to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-advisor who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisors. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. A fund that focuses its investments in the securities of a particular market sector is subject to the risk that adverse circumstances will have a greater impact on the fund than a fund that does not focus its investments in a particular sector. The Fund’s service providers are susceptible to cyber security risks that could result in losses to a Fund and its shareholders. Cyber security incidents could affect issuers in which a Fund invests, thereby causing the Fund’s investments to lose value. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change.

Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the Fund. To obtain a prospectus or a summary prospectus, contact your financial professional or download and/or request one at TouchstoneInvestments.com/resources or call Touchstone at 800.638.8194. Please read the prospectus and/or summary prospectus carefully before investing.

Touchstone Funds are distributed by

Touchstone Securities, Inc.

A registered broker-dealer and member FINRA and SIPC

A member of Western & Southern Financial Group

data.title: IREN: When Large AI Infrastructure Projects Meet A Small-Scale Balance Sheet
data.description:
Summary

Along with CoreWeave (CRWV), Nebius (NBIS), Nscale (Private), Lambda (Private) and Crusoe (Private), IREN (IREN) is one of the leading providers of AI cloud infrastructure in North America.

One of IREN's most important competitive strengths is its 3 GW of contracted power, primarily located in West Texas. The company's Sweetwater data center hub is expected to scale up to 2 GW in the coming years, making it one of the largest AI infrastructure projects in North America.

IREN also differentiates itself with its vertically integrated business model. By owning its own land, data centers, power interconnections and GPUs, IREN is master of its own destiny, thereby reducing counterparty risk.

Despite these competitive strengths, IREN is now focused on one of the most capital-intensive industries that I am aware of. Like CoreWeave and Nebius, IREN is highly dependent upon the debt and equity markets to meet its capital requirements. This leaves the company particularly vulnerable to a shift in sentiment. The artificial intelligence industry is currently awash with liquidity, but that does not mean it will last forever. If and when the music stops, how will IREN meet its massive capital needs associated with its large-scale AI infrastructure projects?

Neocloud Providers Overview

After researching CoreWeave and Nebius over the last few days, I am now turning my attention to IREN, another key player in the AI cloud infrastructure business. What I find interesting is that all of those three neocloud providers were previously focused on cryptocurrency mining. In fact, more than 95% of IREN's revenues last quarter came from Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining activities. However, this is about to flip completely over the next few quarters, as the company is aggressively replacing its AISC chips used for crypto mining with brand-new Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs used in artificial intelligence and machine learning tasks. Once again, IREN is not alone in this transition, as it is only following the steps of its larger peers, as discussed in my previous CoreWeave article.

Founded by Michael Intrator, Brian Venturo, Brannin McBee, and Peter Salanki in September 2017, CoreWeave is a relatively young company. In fact, the CoreWeave Cloud Platform was only launched in 2022, as CRWV was a large-scale Ethereum miner before that.

In September 2025, IREN announced the purchase of an additional 4,200 Blackwell B200 GPUs, bringing the company's total Nvidia GPU fleet to ~8,500 units. As discussed in the most recent earnings call, this number is expected to reach ~140,000 units by the end of 2026. Now, where will all of those GPUs go, and where will the power come from? It is precisely in answering those questions that IREN's distinctive characteristics relative to other neocloud providers become apparent. However, one key question remains: Where will the billions in capital required come from?

IREN's Solid Access To Power

When looking at the picture below, what immediately caught my attention was IREN's Sweetwater data center hub located in West Texas and directly connected to the ERCOT electric grid. With a power capacity of 2 GW across two sites, this is one of the largest AI infrastructure projects that I am aware of. To put Sweetwater's 2 GW power capacity into perspective, Meta Platforms (META) is currently building a 5 GW data center campus called Hyperion in Louisiana. In the interim, Meta also plans to bring online a 1 GW data center cluster called Prometheus sometime in 2026.

Actually, the biggest artificial intelligence project of all in terms of power capacity may well be OpenAI's Stargate program, which could scale up to 10 GW after $500 billion worth of investments. As many of you already know, this is in partnership with Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and Oracle (ORCL), two companies I plan to write about in the coming weeks.

If we come back to IREN, we can see how valuable it is to have 3 GW of secured power in this environment. For comparative purposes, CoreWeave had 2.9 GW of contracted power at the end of September 2025, while Nebius expect 2.5 GW of contracted power by the end of 2026. In short, despite having by far the smallest market capitalization and enterprise value, IREN has the greatest access to power, which is one of the company's greatest strengths.

IREN's Vertically Integrated Model

Another important element that differentiates IREN from other publicly listed GPUs-as-a-service providers like CoreWeave and Nebius is its vertical integration. It is worth keeping in mind that both CRWV and NBIS lease most of their data center space, meaning that their primary tangible assets are their fleet of GPUs. In IREN's case, it is different as the company owns everything in the value chain, including the land, the data center, the power interconnection and the GPUs, among others. This gives IREN's unparalleled control over its operating expenses and also reduces counterparty risks. A great example of counterparty risk within the AI industry would be what happened to CoreWeave last quarter, when it announced delays at one of its data centers due to issues with a third-party contractor.

Microsoft Deal

In early November 2025, IREN probably announced the most important deal in its history, after it secured a $9.7 billion AI cloud contract with Microsoft (MSFT). Under the agreement, IREN will provide Microsoft with access to Nvidia GB300 GPUs over a five-year term. To finance the required infrastructure build-out, IREN will receive a 20% prepayment.

Within the same announcement, the company also disclosed a $5.8 billion deal with Dell Technologies (DELL) for the purchase of GPUs and many other data center components. Microsoft's 20% prepayment will certainly be useful in this regard, and the same could be said about the $1.0 billion in 0.00% convertible senior notes due 2031 issued by IREN in October 2025. Access to capital is both cheap and abundant at the moment, so it is all about growth. In fact, the Microsoft deal alone is expected to result in annualized run-rate revenues of $1.94 billion.

The computing capacity provided to Microsoft will primarily come from IREN's Childress data center facility, also located in West Texas. With a 750 MW power capacity, the Childress hub is obviously much smaller than Sweetwater's 2 GW power capacity, but such a large deal with Microsoft still represents a strong vote of confidence regarding the infrastructure IREN is currently building. As a reminder, Nebius also signed a $17.4 billion deal with Microsoft in September 2025, while CoreWeave generated ~67% of its revenues from MSFT in the third quarter of 2025. Overall, Microsoft has invested $33.0 billion or so in AI cloud infrastructure providers like IREN, Nebius, CoreWeave, Nscale, and Lambda. On that note, both Nscale and Lambda expect to complete their initial public offering sometime in 2026, so the universe of publicly listed neocloud providers is about to get larger.

Balance Sheet

IREN ended September 2025 with $1.03 billion in cash and $963.2 million in convertible notes. With a $68.1 million net cash balance, IREN has a strong balance sheet when compared to CoreWeave's net debt of $11.7 billion. However, based on my current knowledge, Nebius remains the neocloud provider with the strongest balance sheet, given its $688 million net cash balance as of the end of September 2025.

Now, IREN is still relatively early in its AI infrastructure build-out, meaning that the company's capital requirements are about to increase significantly. As of September 2025, IREN owned a fleet of ~8,500 Nvidia GPUs, and this number is expected to reach

~140,000 by the end of 2026. Building a fleet of high-performance GPUs is one thing, by IREN also operates a vertically integrated business model. Vertical integration certainly creates competitive advantages, but it comes at the cost of greater capital intensity relative to a neocloud provider like CoreWeave that is leasing most of its data center space.

The 20% prepayment associated with the Microsoft deal will certainly be useful, but it will be far from enough. IREN also closed a $1.0 billion offering of 0.00% convertible notes in October 2025, which is great. However, given the scale of the company's plan, the debt and equity markets will have to remain wide open for IREN to meet its growth objective.

In the current environment where there are actual shortages in many different segments of the AI supply chain, what is the cost of building a 2 GW AI infrastructure project in a vertically integrated manner? Building a data center hub from scratch is far more capital-intensive than merely renting data center space to install your own GPUs.

Valuation

As mentioned previously, IREN generated more than 95% of its third-quarter revenues from its legacy Bitcoin mining operations, meaning that its most recent financial results do not reflect the company's ongoing shift toward AI cloud services. In this context, we cannot rely on the company's past financial performance as an anchor to look into the future. In terms of actual financial performance, everything remains to be seen, although CoreWeave's and Nebius' financial results may give some clues about what is coming for IREN.

Talking about valuation multiples, IREN is currently trading at 5.0x its 1-year forward sales. This compares to 2.9x for CoreWeave and 7.3x for Nebius. As discussed in my previous Nebius article, I use a price-to-sales ratio simply because most profitability metrics are otherwise negative. When looking at the graph below, CoreWeave's valuation multiple is probably dragged down by its overleveraged balance sheet, while Nebius' premium may reflect its relative balance sheet strength.

Considering its negative operating cash flows and earnings, discussing Nebius' valuation presents some challenges. However, even when using a simple top-line metric like a price-to-sales ratio, Nebius' aggressive valuation and massive embedded growth expectations become obvious.

Generating top-line growth is one thing, but generating actual free cash flows is another thing. What are the returns on capital associated with those tens of thousands of GPUs that are about to be deployed? We are currently in the midst of a historic construction boom, and the amount of new computing power entering the market is just astonishing. Yes indeed, neocloud providers like IREN cannot build capacity fast enough right now, but the risk of overbuilding over the long term remains.

Risk Factor

Capital markets are currently wide open for anything related to artificial intelligence. The historic AI construction boom has been going on for a few years already, and it could continue for quite a while still. In a market environment awash with liquidity, enthusiasm around the prospects of neocloud providers like IREN could continue for a few more quarters or years.

For IREN's share price to come under intense and sustained selling pressure, we will probably have to witness a general withdrawal of liquidity within the AI industry. In turn, this would likely have a serious effect on global markets, given the unprecedented concentration of the S&P 500 at the moment. As of November 24, the ten largest constituents in the S&P 500 represented almost 40% of the index, which is much higher than at any other time in modern history.

Conclusion

With two significant AI infrastructure projects in West Texas, IREN is one of the leading neocloud providers in the market today. The company's Nvidia GPU fleet is expected to grow tremendously in the coming quarters and is expected to reach 140,000 units by the end of 2026.

The company's vertically integrated model certainly creates competitive advantages, but it also increases capital intensity. Given the scale of IREN's projects relative to its balance sheet capacity, capital markets will have to remain wide-open to AI investments if IREN wants to meet its liquidity needs.

My 'Sell' rating reflects the lack of visibility in regards to the profitability of the AI cloud services business. This follows my previous 'Strong Sell' ratings on both CoreWeave and Nebius. With 3 GW of contracted power and a vertically integrated business model, it is my view that IREN is in a better competitive position relative to other neocloud peers.

data.title: Nvidia's Next Surge Is Forming
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) latest earnings don’t just confirm momentum, they redefine it. Its leadership in AI infrastructure solutions and consecutive record-breaking sales in the Blackwell and cloud GPUs reflect the company's long-term outlook. The company experiences high profitability despite growing sales.

Since my last coverage, NVDA has rallied by 4.6%, and the forward-looking thesis is still attractive, with the momentum. Despite the risks posed by the cost issues and supply chain issues, the focus on AI compute workloads still has Nvidia Corporation at the forefront.

Nvidia Data Center Revenue Set to Surpass $65 Billion in Q4FY26

Nvidia's Q3 FY26 holds a lead in Data Center revenue based on massive demand for AI infra. Nvidia had a record Data Center revenue of $51.2 billion for Q3 that represents a 25% QoQ increase from Q2 and a staggering 66% YoY growth. This sector alone accounts for the vast majority of Nvidia's total revenue. The total revenue stood at $57 billion.

I think the growth in Data Center revenue is a major shift for Nvidia with AI at its core. As stated in the call (by the CEO) that "Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out," and emphasize that "compute demand keeps accelerating and compounding across training and inference each growing exponentially." These comments point to a high position for Nvidia as the primary tech enabler of the rapidly expanding AI markets.

Nvidia made strategic deals that solidify this strength more. For instance, the deal with OpenAI to deploy at least 10 gigawatts of Nvidia systems for AI infrastructure. Also, there are deals with industry leaders like Google Cloud, Microsoft, Oracle, and xAI to build AI infrastructure with hundreds of thousands of Nvidia GPUs. I see these deals building an indispensable role in the AI race. Moreover The recent deal with Saudi AI firm Humain to deploy 150,000 accelerators that includes Nvidia's GB300 GPUs, in a Riyadh "AI Zone" further indicates the global reach and major nature of Nvidia's data center products.

The forward trends for Nvidia's Data Center segment are strong. There is a consistent sequential and YoY growth in Data Center revenue across multiple quarters. From Q3FY23 at $3.833 billion to Q3FY26 at $51.215 billion, the topline growth is steep and stable. Moving forward, this sharp growth is supported by Nvidia's Q4FY26 revenue outlook. The Q4 revenue is expected to be $65 billion +/- 2% is in line with the consensus estimate of $65.44 billion.

This forward guidance with management's visibility to $0.5 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue through 2026’s end is a strong indicator of continued topline growth momentum. Nvidia's "A+" quant growth grade with Revenue Growth (YoY) at 71.55% and forward Revenue Growth at 68.93%. Both growth rates are outpacing the sector. The CAGRs (TTM) for Revenue, EBITDA, and Net Income across YoY, 3Y, 5Y, and 10Y periods points to improving-level of performance.

Similarly, Nvidia holds an "A+" quant Profitability Grade with high margins across the metrics compared to the sector median. For instance, the Net Income Margin ((TTM) of 52.41% is vastly exceeding the sector median of 4.64%. This is representing a 10x difference to the sector.

Similarly, the ROCE (TTM) is 109.42% versus the sector median of 6% (17x difference) and Return on Total Assets (TTM) is 61.53% versus the sector median of 2.80% (20x difference). So, these phenomenal profitability metrics are a direct consequence of the high-value/high-demand nature of Nvidia's Data Center products (GPUs and AI platforms). The demand may continue to last in the long-term.

Further, Nvidia's investment in research may continue to fuel this lead, allowing for continuous chip advancement. The scale and ongoing expansion of its AI infra deals, from cloud providers to sovereign states, is difficult for competitors to replicate in the near to medium term.

Nvidia Faces 2026 Margin Risk Amid 553%+ Valuation Premium

The dominance in the data center side by Nvidia cannot be debated, but the valuation issue poses a serious threat. The stock has robust multiples compared to the industry, including 24x sales, compared with the industry average of 3.2x, and 37x book value, compared with the industry average of 3.4x. The forward multiples are still rich. The stock has price-to-earnings ratios that are approximately double the industry averages, and the execution has already factored in perfection. There isn't much room left for error, whether in terms of lower growth, margins, and industry trends.

Therefore, if future performance does not hit or exceed the Street’s elevated expectations. Nvidia's earnings are putting over 700 ETFs on edge as Nvidia stock has become one of the most influential stocks in the ETF ecosystem. NVDA is held in a total of 717 funds with more than 3.5 billion shares in aggregate. This broad exposure means that any negative sentiment/missed expectation can trigger considerable selling across a large swath of investment vehicles (5,600 SEC 13F Holders).

Supply Chain Strains Could Challenge Nvidia’s AI Supercycle

Beyond these valuation concerns, there are inherent supply chain vulnerabilities amid the ongoing shift in memory technology. Nvidia's shift to smartphone-style memory can double server-memory prices by next year-end. It is a critical risk. Why? Nvidia's decision to move to Low-Power Double Data Rate (LPDDR) memory chips for its AI servers may cause server-memory prices to double by late 2026. Nvidia may become a client on the scale of a major smartphone maker. This shift is for the supply chain which may not absorb this scale of demand creating sudden demand that the industry may not handle.

This can potentially lead to tightness at the low end of the market as chipmakers divert factory capacity to LPDDR. Such increased input costs for memory chips can directly impact Nvidia's high gross margins. I think rising input costs and the need to hold gross margins in the mid-70s, in case of a big jump in input cost, can put severe pressure on margin levels that may potentially erode profitability and revise down estimates that have priced in stable high margins.

Furthermore, geopolitical issues and the increasing competition in China are the factors impacting H20 sales. These factors may lead to potential limitations on market access and revenue in main regions. There are concerns about supply catching up with demand, bottlenecks in terms of power, financing, memory, and foundry), and the stability of growth rates.

Nvidia's management projects strong forward revenue, but considering the inherent fragility of a global supply chain for advanced semiconductor tech, in my opinion, any disruption can have a magnified impact on Nvidia’s financials, high demand, and aggressive growth targets. Now, under NVDA stock's elevated valuation, any issue to its supply chain/unexpected increase in costs can trigger a disproportionate negative reaction from the Street and may lead to a considerable decline in stock growth (even a reversal).

Takeaway

From an investment perspective, Nvidia's strong data center trends, record profitability, and rise in AI infrastructure demand signify leadership in the long run. Despite the high valuation, the company's ecosystem, worldwide collaborations, and increased computing demands create a rare setting in the industry where long-run gains could be realized.

data.title: Mining For Opportunity: How Rising AI Demand Is Energizing Uranium And Base Metals
data.description:
The AI infrastructure building boom is fueling global demand for base metals and uranium. Craig Hutchison, Director, Equity Research with TD Cowen joins MoneyTalk to talk about how the mining sector is poised to give investors a diversified way to play the AI wave.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The price for copper and other commodities have hit record highs this year. But will supply issues and demand for AI development be enough to continue that push higher next year? Joining us now to discuss is Craig Hutchison, Director for Equity Research with TD Cowen.

And Craig, thanks for joining us today.

Craig Hutchison: Thanks for having me on the show again.

Anthony Okolie: Alright, we just got through third-quarter earnings. What's the general outlook for the space right now?

Craig Hutchison: I think it's quite constructive for both copper and gold names. I obviously focus mostly on the copper names. I think Q3 results were generally in line with expectations. But I think the setup going into Q4 is much stronger.

We've seen a lift of prices subsequent to Q3, so you're going to benefit from stronger copper prices. As we go into next year, we also think we're going to see stronger copper prices. I'm sure we'll talk about some of the issues around there. We've seen pretty strong supply deficits for the last couple of years, and we see those deficits continuing to '26 and '27, which will obviously support the copper price.

And also, for a lot of our names, most of the production is going to be back-end weighted. So we saw improvement in Q3, and we expect to see an improvement of production into Q4 as well. Some of that's just a function of their mine plans, or grade-driven, et cetera. So I think we're setting up for a stronger Q4, both in terms of the higher copper prices, higher gold prices as well, because a lot of these copper producers have byproduct credits coming from gold. And we'll also see stronger production generally across the board going into Q4.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. So let's walk through some of those specific themes. We'll start with copper. It's hit record prices this year. Are we still seeing some supply disruption in that area?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, so it's been a really interesting year for copper. There's been a lot of disruptions, a lot of volatility. I mean, it's pretty common for mining, and copper in general, to experience a lot of volatility. But if you think back to the start of the year, we had all the threats around tariffs, there was Liberation Day. Everything kind of sold off.

Subsequent to that, we've had a lot of supply disruptions. So Kamoa-Kakula, which is a mine that Ivanhoe (OTCQX:IVPAF) owns, lost about 200,000 tons of supply in the second half of this year, so a pretty big material drop in terms of the-- it's fifth-largest producing mine in the world, so pretty big impact to supply there. You've had Grasberg, which is owned by Freeport (FCX)-- massive supply disruption issue there related to an issue with their block caving. They had a mudslide that came into the actual block cave. That will disrupt that mine well into 2026. So that, again, removed a few hundred thousand dollars tons of supply this year, and a few hundred thousand dollars to next year. El Teniente, which is another mine, Codelco. And then, of course Teck (TECK), which we might talk about later, just in terms MA, they lost some supply disruptions as well.

So you add up all those mines, you've had probably about, across the board, about 6% supply disruption. Normally, in a good year, it's sort of 4% to 5%, so it's been abnormally high level supply disruption, and we're going to see those impacts go into next year. Demand has been OK. It's been sort of GDP 2% to 3% kind of growth. But supply from the mines itself year-over-year is trending closer to 0%. And that's created a very tight market for copper, which will support this year, and obviously into next year, and probably into '27 as well.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Let's talk about the M&A front. Obviously, the Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF)-Teck deal has been big news. It's been in the headlines. Can you give us an update on that specific merger, and whether you expect more M&A in this space going forward?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, it's been very topical. So, there's a vote set for December 9, where shareholders-- there's both A-class shareholders and B-class shareholders will vote. The A-class shareholders is controlled by the Keevils. They've already given support for the deal. So it comes down to the regular, B shareholders will vote on December 9.

I think there was-- since the acquisition, or merger of equals, has been announced, there's been a lot of shareholders who are concerned or question the zero premium offer that's coming from Anglo. So I think there's some frustrated shareholders out there that may not be supporting the deal.

I think most shareholders understand the industrial logic of bringing these two companies together. They're going to have a very strong copper production profile. There are some synergies at one mine, in particular-- QB and Collahuasi-- that will add some value down the road. But I think there is some frustration that shareholders were offered a 0% premium. So it's not a guarantee that the deal goes through.

One of the potential interlopers was BHP. BHP is the largest mining company in the world. You might have seen over the weekend they announced they were trying to come in again for Anglo. They tried last year three times, unsuccessfully. And I guess they've been trying over the last week, unsuccessfully again. So that does remove that deal risk of an interloper coming for Anglo.

I don't think there's a potential interloper from Teck's perspective, just given some of the Canadian government's issues around wants and asks for the Canadian people. And some of those is redomiciled into Canada, or moving to headquarters, and I don't think there's a lot of other people who are willing to move their headquarters to Canada like Anglo is.

So, it'll be interesting. December 9th is the vote. And then for M&A, we haven't seen a huge wave of M&A in the copper space. We've seen more in the gold space. There's just more names to-- but I think it is an ongoing theme.

Certainly, all the majors like Rio (RIO), BHP, Anglo, are all looking to expand their copper production profiles. So they're all looking to target good copper assets out there. So we've seen a little bit. I don't-- and I think going forward, we'll probably continue to see a little bit of that, but I don't think you're going to see a wholesale M&A shift here in the next year or two.

Anthony Okolie: OK. And we'll be watching that December 9th date as well going forward. Now, we've also seen a lot of interest in nuclear and uranium, tied to AI power demand, the transition to renewable energy. What's been playing out in that trend that you see?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, it's been interesting. For the last, I would say, over a year now, the whole nuclear space, or the uranium companies that we cover--Cameco (CCJ), NexGen (NXE), Denison (DNN), UEC -- have all been caught up in the AI trade. They're basically a derivative of the AI trade. Because AI is going to require enormous power demands, and the view is that that power is going to come from nuclear long term.

So we've seen multiples expand over the last year, really got kickstarted around the Constellation Energy (CEG)-Microsoft (MSFT) deal, which I think was announced last September. And so there's been, subsequently, a lot of deals announced between a lot of these hyperscalers. And for the most part it's been SMRs. But more recently, we've started to see some real momentum in the US with Westinghouse and Cameco, and we can obviously talk about that later on in the segment.

But yeah, for sure, there's a very positive tailwind here. AI is putting a lot of power demands onto the grid. The US power demand has actually been growing for the last couple of years. It's been effectively stagnant for 20 years. And so, with the rise of EVs and data centers, et cetera, you are seeing a lot of demand. And there's a question of where that power is going to come from, and nuclear is one of the solutions.

Anthony Okolie: What about trade uncertainty with tariffs? How is that impacting things in the sector?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, I mean, tariffs are obviously a headwind to global GDP growth. We saw those issues back in April and May, there was a lot of uncertainty in terms of what was going to happen. China is still consuming over 50% of the global base metals-- copper, iron ore, et cetera. So any kind of impacts on Chinese demand was going to have an impact on the downstream prices and the outlook for the space in general.

It seems to have quieted down. There seems to be some momentum here in terms of getting some trade resolution. But certainly, as a headwind we've seen more recently in the last couple of weeks, it seems to be that pressure between US and China ease a little bit, and we've seen metal prices rally a bit.

So clearly, again, tariffs are a headwind to global demand. Hopefully, we see those issues kind of quiet down in 2026 and kind of freeze the space for some actual positive growth driven by GDP.

Original Post

data.title: Tesla Execution On Robotics And AI Vision: A Must For Musk
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) stock rallied after CEO Elon Musk pointed out the company’s AI capabilities and custom chip designs. I covered Tesla stock in September with a Hold rating and since then the stock price has increased 19.5% outperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) gain. With prospects in robotics, artificial intelligence and energy storage the company is increasingly focused on positioning Tesla as a company for the future and as a tech company and not so much as a car manufacturer. In this report, I discuss the company’s pivot path, analyze Tesla’s Q3 2025 earnings, and update my price target.

Tesla’s Pivot To Robotics And AI

Tesla’s aim seems to be to pivot to robotics, autonomous solutions and AI. That is a pivot that does make sense. At the same time, we should keep timeline risks and commercialization success in mind. The way Elon Musk does seem to grow companies is through a futuristic view and actually initially missing deadlines initially and failing initially. SpaceX (SPACE) is an example of that. That is not a bad thing, especially if we see how successful SpaceX is today. However, it does mean that we should keep in mind that timelines are not set in stone. Additionally, I do believe that part of the goal setting for Elon Musk’s compensation is driven by wrong indicators including an aspired market capitalization of $8.5 trillion.

Tesla has been working on two key items for quite some time now. Those key elements are FSD, or Full Self-Driving, capabilities, which is also key to autonomous taxi services. The reality is that FSD in current form is more of a Level 2 system that is being incrementally worked on rather than a true autonomous level 4-5 system. At this point, there is not really a supported timeline for unsupervised driving.

Perhaps one of the accelerators for fully functional FSD capabilities can be accelerated through the robotaxi fleet. Robotaxi had a soft launch earlier this year with a Model Y vehicles. In Austin 0.25 million miles have been executed without a driver and in the Bay Area, due to regulations 1 million miles have been executed with a person in the driver seat. The steering wheelless Cybercab is said to enter production in Q2 2026.

On the humanoid robot Optimus is currently scheduled for the end of 2026 with a slow ramp up towards 1 million units. Tesla’s “new projects” can be transformative, especially when coupled to the same neural network and AI, but many of their initiatives have been talked about for years and have a rather slow path to reality.

Tesla Earnings Show Higher Sales, Lower Profit

Analyzing Tesla’s financial results is always a “back to the reality” moment. The results are not bad if we look at the bad image Tesla had gotten earlier this year due to Musk’s political involvement, but they do show that while revenues are up profits are down which is hardly a nice combination.

Tesla’s total sales increased 12% to $28.1 billion with 6% growth in automotive revenues, 44% growth in energy generation and storage revenues and 25% growth in service revenues. Automotive production declined 5%, but deliveries were up 7%. Energy storage deployed grew 81% while super charger station and connectors rose 16% and 18% respectively. Gross profit, however, only grew 1% indicating a margin drop from 19.8% to 18% as vehicle cost increased due to lower fixed cost absorption, sales mix and tariff impacts and regulatory credit revenue decline. Additionally, last year’s figures included a positive impact on sales from one-off revenue recognition for FSD that positively impacted profits as well. Adjusted EBITDA declined 9% to $4.2 billion while free cash flow increased 46% due to a trim on capital expenditures.

Putting everything together, the business is under pressure and Tesla needs the next big thing to drive its results. If you combine the current results with Musk’s compensation back the inherit risks becomes that things may get rushed.

Tesla Stock Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously issued a Hold rating for Tesla stock as there was no upside anymore against expected earnings for 2026. However, given that at the time I issued that Hold rating we were already in the second half of the year perhaps looking at 2027 earnings would have made sense which would have brought the stock price target to $421.57. That target has actually been met and exceeded.

At the same time, I do not feel like that my prior Hold rating was a bad one. We note that estimates on EBITDA and free cash flow have come down substantially. For 2025-2027, EBITDA estimates have been revised down by 6.2% with a 2.1% downward revision for this year and around 7.5% for 2026 and 2027. The CAGR dropped from 12.8% to 9.7%. Free cash flow estimates declined by 9.9%. For this year, driven by downward revision on capital expenditures the estimate actually increased by 30.5% but for 2026 and 2027 the estimates came down by 20.8% and 22.3%. CAGR has dropped from 28.6% to 18.9% and that brings a $408.62 price target.

So, driven by lower expectations for the years ahead the fundamentally supported price target at Tesla’s elevated EV/EBITDA dropped from $421.51 to $408.62. This means that the stock is fairly valued and also provides some rationale on why I previously did not issue a Buy rating.

If we look at the key metrics, we see that the years ahead will be a margin recovery trajectory for Tesla to grow the margins back to around 17%. Capital expenditures remain high at 65 to 75 percent of operating cash flow and the sole positive we are seeing is that the company is in a net cash position. That makes Tesla a company to invest in for its potential robotics and AI pivot driving value into the final years of this decade.

Conclusion: Tesla Valuation Remains Sky High

If we look at Tesla’s current results, we see a clear pressure on the earnings. That also means that Tesla has little to offer at this point in time and the investment case really hinges on the pivot to robotics and AI ramping up in the end of this decade and early next decade. All of that is under the assumption that Musk can actually sustainably execute his vision with set timelines and so far we have seen that it is not really the case. So, we do see possible value on Robotaxi, Optimus and FDS but a lot still needs to happen. Tesla remains a name that still has to live up to its growth expectations.

data.title: SSR Mining: A Rough Q3, But The Gold Miner May Be Set To Shine
data.description:
Is gold setting up for a major year-end rally? I think it could be. Notice in the chart below that the precious metal is in the midst of a bullish symmetrical triangle continuation pattern. The target, based on the depth of the consolidation, is about $4610. Moreover, the only comparable 2025 analog is 1979, and gold soared 33% from Thanksgiving to Christmas that year.

Today, I’m analyzing a small player in the gold mining space. SSR Mining (SSRM) is up 210% so far this year, easily outpacing both gold and the S&P 500. The $4.4 billion market cap company trades at a favorable price-to-earnings ratio but does not pay a dividend. The Colorado-based firm sports strong EPS growth trends and bullish technicals. I have a "Buy" rating on the stock.

Gold: Bullish Year-End Technical Setup

SSR: Strong YTD Alpha

Earlier this month, SSR reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $0.32 was in line with Wall Street expectations, while revenue of $386 million, up 50% from the same period last year, was a material $14 million miss. The company boasted a solid liquidity position as of the end of September, with cash and cash equivalents totaling $409 million.

But shares tumbled 10% in the session that followed, its worst post-earnings performance going back to November 2024. Implied volatility remains high with the gold mining stock at 65%, and the options market prices in a high 11.3% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the Feb. 17 reporting date.

Looking back on the quarter that was, SSR reported an EBITDA miss, driven by lower gold production at Seabee and Marigold. The management team also merely reiterated its 2025 guidance, with gold production now expected to be in the lower half of the previously given range. Unit costs were near the high end of estimates, too. Adding to the negativity was free cash flow in the red due to a working capital build and prepayments at Hod Maden.

On the positive side of the ledger, the firm confirmed that the restart process at its Çöpler mine is on track, and Executive Chairman Rodney Antal said fourth-quarter output should be stronger. Furthermore, development drilling at Buffalo Valley, Porky, and Cortaderas showed promising results, supporting mine life extension initiatives. Overall, full-year gold production is seen in the 410,000 to 480,000 ounce range.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be intense in 2025, with smaller (but still substantial) growth in the out year. By FY 2027, the company could post more than $3.50 of non-GAAP per-share earnings. The top line, meanwhile, should eclipse $2 billion annually over the next handful of quarters.

The sellside remains upbeat on SSR, as evidenced by a high 8 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a lone downgrade. Currently ranked No. 4 out of 276 in its sector by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, profitability trends are decent—free cash flow per share over the past 12 months is now $0.95, a nearly 5% FCF yield.

SSR: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume a long-term normalized annual EPS figure of $3.40 and apply a conservative 8x multiple, then shares should trade near $27. That makes the stock significantly undervalued.

I urge investors not to use a very high P/E, since we are likely closer to a cyclical top in the gold market. Generally, it’s near troughs when high earnings multiples are appropriate, and low P/Es are suitable at cyclical peaks. Shares also trade at a merely fair price-to-sales ratio right now.

SSR: Attractive Earnings-Based Valuation Multiples

Key risks for SSR include lower global gold prices, higher input costs, the threat of natural and operational disasters, increasing regulatory requirements, and unfavorable M&A trends.

At a firm-specific level, the management team has shown a somewhat weak ability to manage costs, so that will be a key story to follow in 2026. Also, the light production forecast calls into question SSR’s underground development strategy.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, Feb. 17 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With gold looking glittery into year-end and SSR’s fundamentals showing mixed indicators, the stock’s technical situation remains constructive. Notice in the chart below that shares tagged long-term support around the $18 level.

Now, there’s apparent resistance very close to the $22 mark. A rally through there could quickly lead to a retest of the $26 high. That peak coincided with a weaker RSI momentum oscillator reading, portending a sharp 30% fall (10 percentage points more than the gold miners ETF, GDX, dropped).

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. I’d like to see SSRM rally above the 50-day moving average, but also like that the RSI has notched a multi-week high.

SSRM: Long-Term Support Held, Rising 200dma, $22 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on SSR. It wasn’t a great Q3 operationally, but the future appears bright, with gold prices potentially rising into the close of 2026.

data.title: Nvidia: You're Running Out Of Time To Sell
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation’s (NVDA) stock trades at a price similar to which it was trading back in August and September, when we issued our latest Sell recommendations on the company. At the same time, since our latest coverage, the risks that Nvidia faces have only increased and now threaten to undermine its dominance over the AI business. Because of that, we have decided to maintain our Sell rating for the company, and in this article, we will highlight why Nvidia’s downside remains significant.

The Market Is Unimpressed

There’s no denying that Nvidia had a good performance in Q3, considering that its revenues during the quarter increased by 62.5% Y/Y to $57 billion and exceeded expectations. Despite such a performance, the company’s stock still depreciated following the release of the report last week and has also underperformed in the last month.

The decline of the share price was caused by the number of things. One of those things is the inability to access the growing Chinese market, which limits Nvidia’s overall upside. During the latest earnings call, Nvidia noted that the sizable purchases of its H20 chips that were developed specifically for the Chinese market have never materialized. The company also didn’t forecast any growth in data center revenues in China in Q4. This is a problem because the Chinese market alone could’ve represented a $50 billion opportunity for Nvidia in 2025. While there are reports that the Trump administration might allow Nvidia to sell even more powerful chips in China, there’s no guarantee that the company would be able to greatly benefit from that decision in Q4 and beyond.

After all, in August, the Chinese state outlets reported that H20 chips are not safe to use. Then, in September, the Chinese regulators banned the purchases of Nvidia’s chips by Chinese companies. One of the reasons behind Beijing’s decision to limit Nvidia’s presence in China is the fact that the country is on its way to becoming self-sufficient in the field of chip design and chip production. The strategists at UBS believe that in five years, the country’s self-sufficiency in the AI computing field could grow to 90%. At the same time, Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang himself initially said in an interview with Financial Times that China would win the AI race.

Considering that a couple of months ago, Huawei shared its 3-year plan to erode Nvidia’s dominance over the AI business, it’s possible that China indeed could become self-sufficient in the AI field over time. That is why it’s hard to see Nvidia expanding its data center business in China anytime soon. With the lack of access to the Chinese market and the rise of Chinese competition, Nvidia’s overall upside in the future could be limited.

At the same time, there’s also a possibility that Nvidia slowly starts to lose market share to competitors back at home. In recent years, hyperscalers have started to design their custom AI chips in order to decrease their reliance on Nvidia and cut costs. In October, Amazon announced that Anthropic, which is the creator of the popular Claude AI chatbot, is going to use 1 million of its custom chips by the end of this year. Then, earlier this week, Elon Musk said that Tesla has now entered the AI chip design business, while Meta is now considering using Google’s AI chips in its data centers in 2027. This suggests that the competition is heating up and there’s no guarantee that Nvidia will be able to continue to dominate the AI market in the upcoming years.

In addition to all of that, there’s also a risk that we’re going to witness the slowdown of capital expenditures on AI, which was responsible for the recent AI boom in the first place, in the foreseeable future. In recent months, the executives of companies like Google, Palantir, and OpenAI have been hinting at the possibility that some spending might be irrational and could yield limited results. They are not entirely wrong, considering that there’s an indication that the majority of companies are currently getting zero return on their artificial intelligence investments. This is why it’s not a surprise that the AI bubble fears are gaining traction and are changing the market sentiment.

The combination of all of the developments described above suggests that the further aggressive growth of Nvidia’s business is not guaranteed anymore. While the company had a good performance in Q3 and will likely end 2025 on a high note, there’s a risk that it could lose its dominance over the AI market in the year to come. This is why a further depreciation of Nvidia’s shares is possible in the foreseeable future.

The Real Value of Nvidia

As risks to Nvidia’s dominance over the AI business increase, we find it hard to justify its current price. The company’s major multiples are already significantly greater in comparison to the sector median multiples, which makes the stock a more risky investment in comparison to others.

In addition, our valuation model also shows that Nvidia is overvalued. When building the model, we used a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is close to the historical GDP and inflation rate. Our discount rate in the model is 12.53%. We figured it out by calculating Nvidia’s cost of debt and cost of equity. When calculating the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4%, a beta of 2.27, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

Thanks to the good performance in Q3 and the improvement of the guidance for Q4, we decided to greatly increase our sales growth rate for 2025 in comparison to the previous model, which is now similar to the consensus growth rate. After 2025, we expect a gradual normalization of the growth rate as the rising risks are likely to prevent Nvidia from growing at a triple-digit rate at which it was expanding its business in the not-so-distant past.

We have also slightly increased the EBIT margin in the model below. In the past, we assumed an EBIT margin of 58%. However, thanks to the good performance in Q3, the TTM EBIT margin is already at nearly 59%. This is why we also increased it in the model below to 59% for 2025. After that, we expect a gradual decrease in margins as the rising challenges that were described in this article earlier could affect the performance of the business. We also maintained the tax rate in the model at 15%, while the bottom part of the table didn’t change significantly in comparison to the previous model, and most of the assumptions there closely correlate with Nvidia’s past performance.

Our model shows that Nvidia’s enterprise value is $2.06 trillion, while its equity value is $2.11 trillion. That means that Nvidia’s intrinsic value is $86.05 per share, which is below the current market price by nearly 52%.

Our previous model from September showed that Nvidia’s intrinsic value is $65.34 per share. The reason why we’re seeing a bump in the intrinsic value in the new model above is that we have greatly increased Nvidia’s sales assumptions, which now closely correlate with the consensus. This was done in order to cover any potential upside that Nvidia might have in case some of our risks don’t materialize. However, even under the revised assumptions, Nvidia remains overvalued, and its stock has plenty of room to fall.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

We see two things that can undermine our bearish thesis. First of all, it’s possible that hyperscalers continue to spend record sums of money on scaling their AI infrastructure in 2026. If that happens, then it might take longer for the risks described in this article to materialize, while in the meantime, Nvidia’s stock might regain its momentum.

Secondly, the potential cut of interest rates in December might improve the market’s mood and change the sentiment for the better again. This could also help Nvidia’s shares to rebound.

However, those things could benefit Nvidia in the short to near-term, only in our opinion. In the long run, the company will likely lose its dominance over the AI market due to the combination of various developments described earlier in this article.

Final Thoughts

The fact that Nvidia’s stock depreciated following the release of the good earnings report for Q3 shows that the market has started to question the company’s ability to continue to dominate the AI business in light of the rising competition. In addition, the inability to access the Chinese market has already limited Nvidia’s overall upside in 2025, and that’s unlikely to change in 2026 and beyond, considering that Beijing aims to become more self-sufficient in the field of chip design and chip production. Also, the rising AI bubble fears have already derailed Nvidia’s stock momentum, while the potential AI CapEx bust could result in a significant depreciation of the company’s shares in the foreseeable future.

Considering all of those developments, we believe that maintaining the rating of Sell for Nvidia’s stock makes the most sense right now. This is especially true since the company is already overvalued at the current price.

data.title: Madison Diversified Income Fund Q3 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Recap - Third Quarter 2025

For all intents and purposes, the third quarter of 2025 was another exceptional period for investors, the S&P 500 was up another 8.1%, international stocks (MSCI ACWI ex-US Index) gained 6.9%, and bonds (Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index) tacked on 2.0%. However, despite the handsome absolute gains all-around, for those light on mega cap growth stocks, it felt like another “AI or die” quarter from a relative standpoint as the Magnificent-7 (Mag-7) and other AI associated stocks vastly outperformed in a continuation of the market action we’ve seen since the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022. The return differential was stark, the Mag-7 averaged a near 18.0% return versus the median S&P 500 stock up just 4.4%. Dividend-focused stocks were even further behind, with the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index returning just 4.0%.

Also notable was the performance of higher beta and lower quality stocks, evidenced by the 13.0% return on the S&P 500 High Beta Index (now up 64.0% off the April low!) and the 12.4% showing from the small cap Russell 2000 Index. Quality stocks were left behind in the quarter’s momentum thrust with the S&P 500 Quality Index up a solid, yet relatively pedestrian 3.1%.

Markets were further boosted by shifting expectations for easier monetary policy (interest rate cuts) from the Federal Reserve (Fed). July’s jobs report, released on August 1st, disappointed to the low side, but the kicker was in the large negative revisions to the May and June figures, where over 250,000 jobs were wiped away. The negative shock cemented a 0.25% rate cut in September and pushed up odds for an additional 0.50% of rate cuts over the remainder of the year. Interest rates fell and rate sensitive stocks, like small caps, jumped with 10.5% (85%) of the Russell 2000 Index’s 12.4% return coming in August/September. The fall in rates was even more beneficial to bonds, where 2.3% (113%) of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index’s 2.0% return was made in the final two months of the quarter.

Performance

The Madison Diversified Income Fund (MBLAX) trailed the benchmark for the quarter but remains well ahead of the bogey for 2025.

Equity performance was held back from underperformance by our covered call and MLP positions, somewhat offset by outperformance from our core active dividend equity holding.

Within fixed income, strong performance from our holdings in investment grade corporate bonds and mortgage-backed securities boosted performance. Finally, our active aggregate bond holding was an outperformer, while our active short-term position underperformed.

Outlook

As we move into 2025’s final act, we marvel at the S&P 500 Index’s astonishing 35% run off the April low. A ricochet trajectory not typically seen outside the aftermath of a violent bear market. It appears we’ve reached a point where investors’ level of exuberance can only be rivaled by their level of cognitive dissonance. We commented last quarter that markets have been trading like uncertainty has been eradicated, yet it most certainly remains. The economic distortions of the tariff saga are immense, and the ripple effects are only now just starting to come to light as the deliberations and trade deals carried on until early August. Investors are all-in on AI, yet there is little evidence that the hundreds of billions of dollars in capital thrown at the industry have a viable path to substantial revenue generation.

The Fed’s focus has pivoted away from price stability to a weakening labor market despite inflation reaccelerating, continuing to increase at a rate 50% above their target, and their collective wisdom not seeing a return to 2% until 2028. The U.S. equity market trading at nearly 23x 12 months forward earnings doesn’t appear to believe much, if anything can go wrong. The same can be seen in the corporate bond market where interest rate spreads over U.S. Treasuries (credit spreads) sit near historic lows.

Gold continues to send a cautionary message, up 16% in the quarter and has gained over 40% in 2025 alone. Investors typically turn to the precious metal for crisis insurance and/or a hedge against inflation/currency depreciation. Clearly the shifting geopolitical landscape and deteriorating U.S. government financial position, with no signs of fiscal restraint, are spooking a large contingent of market participants. We heed the message.

Resilient consumer spending is holding recession risk in check. There is no doubt that the labor market has slowed, but it’s not showing undue signs of stress. Turnover has stalled, with very little hiring or firing happening in the aggregate. To us, this is another sign that uncertainty remains in the minds of business leaders awaiting better clarity on the economy. However, until we see a material rise in unemployment claims, the U.S. economy will likely remain stable.

In conclusion, it should be clear that we don’t share the market’s view of a goldilocks future, yet we’re cautiously optimistic. We see pockets of the U.S. equity market as teetering on irrational exuberance, but there are opportunities in companies beyond the AI sphere where valuations are less demanding. Given the uncertain backdrop, elevated valuations, and investor overconfidence, we continue to stress higher quality assets and remain calculating in taking risk. True to our participate and protect philosophy, we don’t think it’s a good time to be to be swinging for the fence.

Respectfully,
